did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Networks (ACGAN), Progressive ACGAN, and the proposed Auxiliary Classifier Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network (ACStyleGAN), to synthesize skin lesion images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. These AI-generated images were used for data augmentation to improve the performance of a ResNet50 classifier in dermatology tasks such as skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel deep learning architecture (ACStyleGAN) for skin lesion image generation. It details specific methodology (dataset preprocessing, model architecture, training parameters), reports original results (classification metrics for ResNet50 on augmented vs. original data), and includes figures of synthesized images. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICARC), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: ACStyleGAN for Skin Lesion Image Generation
journal: 5th International Conference on Advanced Research in Computing (ICARC)
brief_summary: This study addresses class imbalance in skin lesion classification by generating synthetic dermoscopy images using the proposed Auxiliary Classifier Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network (ACStyleGAN). Utilizing the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven lesion classes, the authors integrated Adaptive Instance Normalization and noise injection into a progressive training framework to enhance image realism. The synthesized images were used to augment the original dataset, and the resulting performance was evaluated using a ResNet50 classifier. Comparison of classification metrics revealed that the ACStyleGAN-augmented dataset improved accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision over the original data. Specifically, the augmented model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.9502, outperforming the baseline accuracy of 0.9465. The results indicate that ACStyleGAN effectively mitigates class imbalance and enhances classifier performance compared to basic ACGAN and Progressive ACGAN approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for synthesis and classification. Augmented dataset contained 200 images.

all_task_types.target: Synthetic dermoscopy images of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The primary task of the proposed ACStyleGAN model is to generate synthetic skin lesion images to address class imbalance. The model synthesizes images conditioned on seven specific lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, MEL, VASC) from the ISIC 2018 dataset. The generated images are 256x256 pixels.

A ResNet50 classifier was used as an evaluation metric to assess the quality of the generated images. The classifier predicts discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes) on both original and augmented datasets. This is a secondary evaluation task, not the primary generative task of the paper's proposed model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images for skin lesions, hosted by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains images belonging to 7 different classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used as the source dataset for generating synthetic images via ACStyleGAN, Progressive ACGAN, and Basic ACGAN. The original images were resized to 256x256 and normalized.
use.testing: The original dataset was used to establish a baseline for the ResNet50 classifier. The augmented dataset (original + synthesized images) was used to evaluate the ResNet50 classifier's performance.
use.other: Synthesized images were generated for each class to augment the dataset: MEL (150), BCC (50), AKIEC (200), BKL (300), DF (200), VASC (30). The total number of synthesized images is 980.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the original and augmented datasets were made using descriptive classification metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) without statistical significance testing.

overall_classification_metrics.description: Classification metrics for ResNet50 on original vs. ACStyleGAN-augmented datasets.
overall_classification_metrics.notes: The augmented dataset contained synthesized images: MEL (150), BCC (50), AKIEC (200), BKL (300), DF (200), VASC (30). The ACStyleGAN method improved performance across all overall metrics compared to the original dataset.

class_wise_classification_metrics.description: Classification metrics for ResNet50 on specific lesion classes for original vs. augmented datasets.
class_wise_classification_metrics.notes: Most classes benefited from augmentation in terms of classification accuracy, specificity, and precision. Notable improvements were seen in AKIEC (accuracy 96.69% to 97.22%) and BCC (accuracy 95.63% to 97.02%). Recall for DF improved significantly from 59.09% to 68.18%.

comparison_with_previous_work.description: Comparison of overall classification metrics with previous work (Qin et al. [10]).
comparison_with_previous_work.notes: The overall classification metrics achieved using ACStyleGAN are comparable to Qin et al. [10]. The accuracy of ACStyleGAN exceeds the overall accuracy obtained by Kaur et al. [11], though sensitivity and precision are lower than Kaur et al.

architecture_used.main_model: ACStyleGAN (Auxiliary Classifier Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network)

generator.noise_injection: Applied after AdaIN (reversed order from original StyleGAN)
generator.conditioning: Class labels embedded and combined with noise vector

loss_functions.loss_weighting: Dynamic adaptive loss weighting (auxiliary weight increases, adversarial weight decreases with epochs)

training_details.epochs: Dynamic epochs per resolution (no fixed total reported)

classification_or_output_head.description: Two separate output layers in the discriminator

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Auxiliary Classifier Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network (ACStyleGAN)
Skin Lesion Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network (SL-StyleGAN)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Generated synthetic skin lesion images using the proposed Auxiliary Classifier Style-Based Generative Adversarial Network (ACStyleGAN). The model synthesized images for specific classes to augment the dataset: Melanoma (MEL) - 150 images, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) - 50 images, Actinic Keratoses (AKIEC) - 200 images, Benign Keratosis (BKL) - 300 images, Dermatofibroma (DF) - 200 images, and Vascular Lesions (VASC) - 30 images. Total synthesized images: 980.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic Data Generation via Progressive ACGAN
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Generated synthetic images using a Progressive Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) architecture. This method was evaluated as an intermediate augmentation approach but was found to produce less realistic images compared to ACStyleGAN.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic Data Generation via Basic ACGAN
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Generated synthetic images using a basic Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) architecture. Trained for 20 epochs with a gradient penalty term of 40. This method resulted in checkerboard artifacts and was less effective than ACStyleGAN.

Used synthetic image generation to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset. The number of synthesized images per class was determined by the scarcity of original data, with larger augmentations applied to underrepresented classes (e.g., BCC, VASC) and larger classes (e.g., BKL, NV).

conclusions: The proposed ACStyleGAN architecture effectively mitigates class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 skin lesion dataset by generating high-quality, class-specific synthetic dermoscopy images.

ACStyleGAN outperforms both basic ACGAN and Progressive ACGAN in terms of image realism, as basic ACGAN produced checkerboard artifacts and Progressive ACGAN failed to produce realistic lesions for certain classes.
Data augmentation using ACStyleGAN-generated images improved the overall performance of a ResNet50 classifier compared to using the original dataset, increasing accuracy from 0.9465 to 0.9502.
The ACStyleGAN-augmented model showed improvements across all evaluated metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision) relative to the baseline model trained on original data.
The classification performance achieved with ACStyleGAN is comparable to previous work by Qin et al. (accuracy 0.952) and exceeds the overall accuracy reported by Kaur et al.

The study demonstrates that integrating Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN) and noise injection into a progressive training framework enhances the stability and quality of GAN-based medical image synthesis.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which is a single-source dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets to assess generalizability across different populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report any formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance improvements; comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision).

The number of synthesized images per class was determined by the scarcity of original data (e.g., 50 for BCC, 30 for VASC), which may introduce bias if the synthetic samples do not adequately capture the intra-class variance of the rare classes.

The ResNet50 classifier was trained and evaluated on the same dataset split (original vs. augmented) without mention of cross-validation or independent test sets, raising potential concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific augmentation strategy.

The improvement in classification metrics is modest (e.g., accuracy increased from 0.9465 to 0.9502), and the performance is comparable to previous work (Qin et al.), suggesting that the novel ACStyleGAN architecture offers incremental rather than transformative gains.

The basic ACGAN baseline suffered from checkerboard artifacts, indicating that the choice of generator architecture is critical for medical image synthesis, but the paper does not provide a detailed quantitative analysis of image quality (e.g., FID or IS scores) beyond visual inspection and downstream classification performance.

The training used dynamic epochs per resolution and adaptive loss weighting, which are complex hyperparameters; the paper does not provide a sensitivity analysis or ablation study to demonstrate the robustness of these specific training choices.
